```text
<!-- Source: https://medium.com/design-bootcamp/the-secret-to-creating-neutral-color-palettes-5e5a650b1718 -->
# The secret to creating neutral color palettes
*By Pablo Figueiredo*
---
## A story of when a work assignment became an investigation for a different way of creating a neutral color palette.
[
![Pablo Figueiredo](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*AAteobW6YZw9iuolLJx85g.png)
](https://medium.com/@pablofig?source=post_page---byline--5e5a650b1718---------------------------------------)
8 min read
Jan 21, 2023
\--
Press enter or click to view image in full size
## What motivated me
It all started when I was working on a Design System and defining its palette of neutral colors, those used mainly in texts and less prominent elements, but I wanted to explore another alternative to the classic pure gray, because I saw there an opportunity to enhance the existing visual identity and give a unique character to its applications.
The expected result was a neutral palette ranging from the lightest to the darkest gray, in which all colors slightly followed the same tone of blue, harmonizing with the institutional blue in a silvery hue. The solution seemed easy, adding a light tint of blue in a pure gray palette, following the same hue as the brand’s blue.
Press enter or click to view image in full size
To create the palette, I chose to use the HSL color pattern which is composed of three variables: hue, saturation and lightness, and manipulating them to get the desired result seemed simple, lightness values between 10 and 90 would be selected, the hue value would be the same as the institutional blue, and finally I would find the ideal saturation value so that the blue hue is discreetly perceived in the different grays.
## The problem that showed up
However, as it was to be expected from the title, that was not how it happened.
Press enter or click to view image in full size
When I tested this solution, I noticed that something seemed wrong, when the saturation was low, around 20, the midtones reached the desired shade, however the light and dark shades did not appear to have the same intensity. This difference is noticeable in the lighter sahdes, they seem to be closer to their 0 saturation equivalents, while the mid shades appear to be bluish, in particular the middle color, which appears much more bluish and intense than its neighboring colors.
Press enter or click to view image in full size
On the other hand, when the saturation reached higher values, around 40, the light and dark shades reached the desired shade, however the medium shades appeared to be much more pigmented than the rest. In this other palette, the mentioned divergences seem even more evident.
I tested different saturation values, on the HSL and HSB scales, but the problem continued and the edges of the palette always seemed to be washed out when compared to the center. This problem compromised the applicability of the neutral palette, because the hierarchy of tones became inconsistent due to some medium shades appearing to be more saturated and gaining prominence over darker tones, for example. The palette should be linear, just like the one with 0 saturation.
## How I tried to fix the problem
My first instinct was to correct it using a not so recommended tool, the eyeball, it was very difficult to achieve a cohesive result, because every time I manipulated the values of a color, it seemed that the problem was in another color, so I would mess with that one, then another one would stand out, I kept testing alternatives, changing not only the saturation, but the lightness and hue as well. Anyway, I got a messy and chaotic result in which all the values vary irregularly, but still seemed more harmonious than the original.
Press enter or click to view image in full size
I believe I ended up convincing myself that this result was sufficient after a lot of repetitive work and getting used to it, that is, that famous effect that sabotages the designer’s visual impressions of his own work. Looking back now, I realize that I still wasn’t clear about the problem with the previous palette, I had no clue it could be corrected only by the saturation, and I ended up getting lost in a challenge that eludes us by nature, the color perception.
## Understanding the problem
I was certainly not satisfied with the result, but I couldn’t find a solution to this problem, until at one point I stopped paying attention to the numerical fields of the HSL values and noticed something curious about the visual selector.
This was the image in Figma’s color picker, the hue is always the same and their shades vary according to saturation on the horizontal axis and lightness on the vertical axis. However, I couldn’t help but notice that its variations didn’t seem to follow the horizontal and vertical variations evenly.
After noticing this discrepancy, I made an arbitrary delimitation according to my perception of the dominant shades into two groups: on the left side were predominantly gray shades, and on the right side mostly light and dark blue shades. This division is arbitrary, however I found it curious that all the alternative boundaries I made followed the same curved shape and none of them were completely straight.
I then realized the connection between this format and the problem I encountered when assembling the scales with fixed saturation, that the closer a color was to the ends, the more its shade diverged, just as the ends of this line diverge from its central point forming an arc.
That is, as unintuitive as it may seem, perhaps the best path to a linear and cohesive scale was not a straight line with fixed saturation, but a curved path. I already knew a technique for balancing lightness and saturation to create light and dark shades of a color for secondary uses, yet nothing similar to what I used to arrive at the desired neutral palette.
## Understanding the solution
When analyzing these elements, I came up with a mathematical comparison, in which the color picker image could be understood as a Cartesian plane with its vertical and horizontal axes (lightness and saturation) divided into 100 units, while the curve I saw was nothing more than a parabola represented geometrically in the plane.
In this context, it would be possible to discover which function produces the parabola that I was visualizing within that plane of saturation and lightness. This formula would be the main tool for generating a harmonic palette, because I would be able to apply a desired lightness value and the result of this formula would be the saturation value corresponding to the color that is under the dotted line of the parabola.
## Building the solution
First of all, I needed to rescue some knowledge from school days, but I don’t intend to go into much detail about the elements of the parables, just about what is necessary to understand the construction of the new palette.
Based on what I studied, I was able to position the parabola with its opening facing right and align its vertex to the center of the plane, equivalent to the saturation value of 50. As usual, I used the variable “x” to represent the horizontal axis and the variable “y” to represent the vertical axis.
After testing several alternatives, the final formula was found, but it was not as simple as it seemed in the previous GIF, even with the help of online tools I could not directly manipulate the shape of the parabola to obtain its function, I needed to change the function values to modify the parabola, so I needed to understand what kind of manipulation would result in exactly the change I was looking for. To test the color palette resulting from this formula, I needed to substitute the “x” value for each desired lightness value, so I did the math and got the corresponding saturation value for each shade of the scale.
## The result
After getting frustrated trying to correct the palette just by looking at it, I was a little suspicious if this new technique would work, I was only sure that the result wasn’t a disaster when I visualized the complete palette and compared it with the previous one.
Press enter or click to view image in full size
It was clear to me that the palette that varied its saturation according to the parabola has a more defined linearity compared to the fixed saturation palette, correcting the main problem of losing its tonality at the ends and ensuring a better defined hierarchy between light and dark shades.
Looking deeper into the palette, I realize that I arrived at an approximated formula, which is not perfect, but still solves the problem of the previous one and is a more adequate solution to fulfill the needs of a neutral palette in my project.
Press enter or click to view image in full size
It’s hard not to be influenced by the colors of this text’s formatting when looking at the palette’s application image, but it’s noticeable that in this context the neutral shades don’t appear to be as pigmented as when we visualize them in the palette outside of their application. The applied result fulfilled the goal, bringing a final piece that does not need a complex visual composition of colors to bring its identity, since the neutral tones themselves have authenticity and differ from the most used pattern of achromatic tones.
## What I understood from the problem
All this showed me that predicting chromatic interactions is not an easy task and that not always the best way is to blindly trust the values organized by the digital systems that we have in editing software. I have tried to investigate further the problem of divergence in tonality and have not found a satisfactory explanation about why this happens, but I believe that my story and solution still have their contribution.
I was very happy with the result, but mainly with the discovery of this process that was unknown to me and my colleagues. I believe that this technique is not for every type of project, because I don’t see it as a substitute for the classic neutral palettes in pure grayscale, but rather an alternative for building a visual system, which facilitates the recognition of its identity in applications with less supporting graphic elements.
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-6 2026-03-13 09:35:05Z)**_

Here's a breakdown of the article's key insights:

**Core Problem:** When creating neutral palettes with a fixed HSL saturation, the chromatic intensity is uneven across lightness. At low saturation (~20), midtones look correct but the light/dark extremes appear washed out, nearly indistinguishable from pure grey. At high saturation (~40), the extremes look right but midtones become visibly too blue/pigmented. There's no single saturation value that works across the full range.

**Key Insight:** The author noticed in Figma's color picker that the boundary between "grey-looking" and "blue-looking" shades isn't a straight line — it follows a curve. The further from mid-lightness, the more saturation diverges. This means a linear (fixed) saturation approach is fundamentally mismatched with how color perception works.

**Solution:** Treat the saturation/lightness picker as a Cartesian plane and plot a parabola through it. The vertex sits at lightness 50 (where the least saturation is needed) and opens outward toward both extremes (where more saturation is needed). For each lightness value, the formula outputs the corresponding saturation that keeps perceived chromatic intensity consistent.

**Narrative Arc:**
1. Design system work needed tinted neutrals (blue-silver)
2. Tried fixed HSL saturation — noticed uneven results
3. Tried eyeballing corrections — chaotic, unconvincing
4. Noticed the curved boundary in Figma's color picker
5. Connected that curve shape to a parabola
6. Built a formula, tested it, got a cohesive result
7. Compared fixed vs curved — clear improvement in linearity

**Terminology:** The author frames it as "linearity" — a well-constructed neutral palette should feel linear in its tonal progression, and the parabolic curve achieves that where fixed saturation cannot.

This maps directly to your modal's structure. Want me to refine the modal copy based on any of these insights?

Base directory for this skill: /Users/grant/Documents/Projects/BoardPM/plugin/skills/add

# Add Board Item

Create a new item in the `board/` project management system. The system spec is bundled with the plugin at `/Users/grant/Documents/Projects/BoardPM/plugin/spec/board-system-spec.md`.

## Existing Board Items

Pre-loaded board data for duplicate detection:

filepath	type	status	priority	blocked_by	parent	title

Before creating, check if a similar item already exists. If a close match is found, ask the user if they still want to create a new one.

## Scope Assessment

When the user requests a **TASK**, evaluate whether it is actually EPIC-sized before creating it. Look for these signals:

- Multiple distinct steps or deliverables in the description
- Multiple action verbs joined by "and" (e.g., "add auth and set up roles and integrate SSO")
- Work that spans different code areas or concerns
- Sub-steps that could be completed and shipped independently

**Quick test:** "Could someone meaningfully complete and ship part of this while the rest remains undone?" If yes → likely an EPIC.

If EPIC signals are present, suggest an EPIC + sub-TASKs with a concrete breakdown. For example:

> "This looks like it has multiple independent deliverables. Want me to create an EPIC with separate TASKs for each piece instead?"

**This is a soft suggestion only.** If the user says "just make it a task," comply without pushback.

This assessment only applies to TASK creation — do not second-guess EPICs, NOTEs, or other types.

## Arguments

The user provides the type and title: `/board:board-add task <title>`, `/board:board-add epic <title>`, etc.

If no type is given, ask the user what type of item to create.

## Determine Next ID

Run the ID assignment script:

```bash
bash /Users/grant/Documents/Projects/BoardPM/plugin/scripts/next-id.sh <TYPE>
```

This scans all directories (including `board/archive/` and `.doc-ids`) and returns the next available 4-digit zero-padded ID.

## Create the File

Use the correct directory, frontmatter, and naming convention:

| Type | Directory | Filename Example |
|------|-----------|-----------------|
| task | `board/tasks/` | `TASK-0014_add-batch-processing.md` |
| epic | `board/planning/` | `EPIC-0009_batch-mode.md` |
| adr | `board/planning/` | `ADR-0002_chose-webgpu-over-wasm.md` |
| note | `board/planning/` | `NOTE-0001_explore-wasm-option.md` |
| reference | `board/reference/` | `REFE-0007_webgpu-overview.md` |
| resource | `board/resources/` | `RESO-0001_webgpu-article.md` |

## Frontmatter Templates

Task:
```yaml
---
type: task
status: backlog
priority: 2
created: YYYY-MM-DD
---
```

Epic:
```yaml
---
type: epic
status: planning
created: YYYY-MM-DD
---
```

ADR:
```yaml
---
type: adr
status: active
context_date: YYYY-MM-DD
created: YYYY-MM-DD
---
```

Note:
```yaml
---
type: note
status: inbox
created: YYYY-MM-DD
---
```

Reference:
```yaml
---
type: reference
created: YYYY-MM-DD
---
```

Resource (ask user for URL):
```yaml
---
type: resource
status: inbox
url: <url>
created: YYYY-MM-DD
---
```

## Naming Rules

- Convert title to kebab-case for filename
- Task titles MUST be imperative mood (start with a verb). If the user gives a noun phrase, rewrite it (e.g., "batch processing" -> "Add batch processing support")
- Use today's date for `created`

## After Creation

Show the file path and ask if the user wants to add more detail to the body.


ARGUMENTS: the knowledge from that article into a REFE item

```md
# Parabolic Saturation Curve for Neutral Palettes

Source: [The secret to creating neutral color palettes](https://medium.com/design-bootcamp/the-secret-to-creating-neutral-color-palettes-5e5a650b1718) by Pablo Figueiredo (Jan 2023)

## Problem

When building a neutral palette with a consistent hue (e.g. blue-tinted greys), using a fixed HSL saturation value produces uneven chromatic intensity across lightness:

- **Low saturation (~20):** Midtones look correct, but light and dark extremes lose nearly all visible chroma — they collapse toward pure grey.
- **High saturation (~40):** Extremes recover their tint, but midtones become visibly over-saturated and break the neutral hierarchy.

No single fixed saturation value produces consistent perceived chroma across the full lightness range. Manual eyeball correction leads to chaotic, irregular values.

## Key Insight

In a saturation/lightness plane (e.g. Figma's color picker), the perceptual boundary between "grey" and "chromatic" is not a straight vertical line — it follows a curve. Colours at the extremes of lightness need more saturation to register as tinted, while midtones need less. This curve is approximately parabolic.

## Solution

Model saturation as a function of lightness using a parabola:

- Vertex at mid-lightness (minimum saturation needed)
- Opens toward both ends (increasing saturation at light and dark extremes)
- For each lightness value, the formula outputs the saturation that maintains consistent perceived chroma

This replaces a single saturation constant with a lightness-dependent curve, producing palettes where tonal progression feels linear and no part of the scale is sacrificed.

## Design Context

- The technique is specifically for **tinted neutrals** (not pure achromatic greys) — palettes that carry a subtle hue while remaining functionally neutral.
- Intended for design systems where neutral colours carry brand identity (e.g. blue-silver tones harmonizing with an institutional blue).
- The result is an approximation — perceptually good, not mathematically perfect — but dramatically better than fixed saturation.
- The author positions this as an alternative to pure greyscale, not a replacement.

```

```text
     1→import chroma from "chroma-js";
     2→import type { ColorEntry, PaletteParams } from "../types/palette";
     3→
     4→/**
     5→ * Parabolic saturation curve centered at lightness 50%.
     6→ * See: https://www.desmos.com/calculator/02ufrfsuzy
     7→ */
     8→export function getSaturation(lightness: number, sMod: number): number {
     9→  const o = 50;
    10→  return 1 + (Math.pow(lightness - o, 2) / sMod - Math.pow(o, 2) / sMod) / 100;
    11→}
    12→
    13→export function generateNeutrals(params: PaletteParams): ColorEntry[] {
    14→  const { hue, saturation, sMod } = params;
    15→  const min = 0.03;
    16→  const max = 1.01;
    17→  const distance = 0.01;
    18→
    19→  const entries: ColorEntry[] = [];
    20→  let value = min;
    21→  let step = 0;
    22→
    23→  while (value < max) {
    24→    const i = Math.floor(value * 100);
    25→    const sK = getSaturation(i, sMod);
    26→    const color = chroma.hsl(hue, saturation * sK, value);
    27→
    28→    const [h, s, l] = color.hsl();
    29→    const hRound = Math.round(h) || 0;
    30→    const sRound = Math.round(Math.round((s + Number.EPSILON) * 100 * 100) / 100);
    31→    const lRound = Math.round(Math.round((l + Number.EPSILON) * 100 * 100) / 100);
    32→
    33→    entries.push({
    34→      label: `grey-${i}`,
    35→      lightness: i,
    36→      hsl: [h, s, l],
    37→      hslString: `${hRound}deg ${sRound}% ${lRound}%`,
    38→      hex: color.hex(),
    39→    });
    40→
    41→    step++;
    42→    value = step * distance + min;
    43→  }
    44→
    45→  return entries.reverse();
    46→}
    47→
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (2026-03-13 09:41:00Z)**_

yes use 20 and 40. and maybe add a 0 sat strip above. also the article points out the swatches that are the desired shade in each strip which I like, how might we highlight the desired shades in our strips?

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-6 2026-03-13 09:41:11Z)**_

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-6 2026-03-13 09:41:16Z)**_

A few options for highlighting the "correct" swatches:

1. **Height bump** — correct swatches are taller (e.g. `h-12`) while the rest stay `h-10`, creating a subtle stepped silhouette
2. **Bottom tick marks** — small dots or lines beneath the correct swatches
3. **Top border** — a thin amber or white line on the top edge of the correct swatches
4. **Dimming the wrong ones** — overlay a semi-transparent dark layer on the "off" swatches so the correct ones pop by contrast

For the strips:
- **0% sat**: no highlights (pure baseline)
- **20% fixed**: highlight the middle swatches (where chroma reads correctly)
- **40% fixed**: highlight the extremes (where chroma reads correctly)
- **Curved**: highlight all (the whole strip is balanced)

I'd lean toward option 1 (height bump) or 4 (dimming) — both communicate "these are the good ones" without extra UI clutter. What do you think?

```text
    69→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    70→// Curve + formula figure (compact, shown below the explanation)
    71→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    72→
    73→function CurveAndFormula() {
    74→  const w = 480;
    75→  const h = 140;
    76→  const pad = { l: 40, r: 12, t: 16, b: 24 };
    77→  const pw = w - pad.l - pad.r;
    78→  const ph = h - pad.t - pad.b;
    79→
    80→  const points: { x: number; y: number }[] = [];
    81→  for (let l = 0; l <= 100; l += 2) {
    82→    points.push({ x: l, y: getSaturation(l, REF_SMOD) });
    83→  }
    84→  const allY = points.map((p) => p.y);
    85→  const yMin = Math.min(...allY) * 0.92;
    86→  const yMax = Math.max(...allY) * 1.06;
    87→
    88→  const toX = (l: number) => pad.l + (l / 100) * pw;
    89→  const toY = (v: number) => pad.t + (1 - (v - yMin) / (yMax - yMin)) * ph;
    90→
    91→  const d = points
    92→    .map(
    93→      (p, i) =>
    94→        `${i === 0 ? "M" : "L"}${toX(p.x).toFixed(1)},${toY(p.y).toFixed(1)}`,
    95→    )
    96→    .join("");
    97→
    98→  const baselineY = toY(1);
    99→
   100→  return (
   101→    <div className="rounded-xl border border-white/[0.06] bg-white/[0.02] overflow-hidden">
   102→      <svg viewBox={`0 0 ${w} ${h}`} className="w-full h-auto">
   103→        {/* Baseline */}
   104→        <line
   105→          x1={pad.l}
   106→          y1={baselineY}
   107→          x2={w - pad.r}
   108→          y2={baselineY}
   109→          stroke="rgba(255,255,255,0.1)"
   110→          strokeWidth={1}
   111→          strokeDasharray="4 3"
   112→        />
   113→        <text
   114→          x={pad.l - 6}
   115→          y={baselineY + 3}
   116→          textAnchor="end"
   117→          className="text-[8px] fill-white/25 font-mono"
   118→        >
   119→          1.0x
   120→        </text>
   121→
   122→        {/* Fill under curve */}
   123→        <path
   124→          d={`${d} L${toX(100).toFixed(1)},${(pad.t + ph).toFixed(1)} L${toX(0).toFixed(1)},${(pad.t + ph).toFixed(1)} Z`}
   125→          fill="url(#aboutCurveFill)"
   126→        />
   127→        <defs>
   128→          <linearGradient id="aboutCurveFill" x1="0" y1="0" x2="0" y2="1">
   129→            <stop
   130→              offset="0%"
   131→              stopColor="hsl(38 90% 55%)"
   132→              stopOpacity="0.1"
   133→            />
   134→            <stop
   135→              offset="100%"
   136→              stopColor="hsl(38 90% 55%)"
   137→              stopOpacity="0.01"
   138→            />
   139→          </linearGradient>
   140→        </defs>
   141→
   142→        {/* Curve */}
   143→        <path
   144→          d={d}
   145→          fill="none"
   146→          stroke="hsl(38 90% 55%)"
   147→          strokeWidth={2}
   148→          strokeLinecap="round"
   149→          strokeLinejoin="round"
   150→        />
   151→
   152→        {/* Annotations */}
   153→        <text
   154→          x={toX(50)}
   155→          y={toY(getSaturation(50, REF_SMOD)) + 14}
   156→          textAnchor="middle"
   157→          className="text-[9px] fill-white/25 font-mono"
   158→        >
   159→          min at L=50
   160→        </text>
   161→
   162→        {/* X axis */}
   163→        <text
   164→          x={toX(0)}
   165→          y={h - 4}
   166→          className="text-[8px] fill-white/15 font-mono"
   167→        >
   168→          dark
   169→        </text>
   170→        <text
   171→          x={toX(50)}
   172→          y={h - 4}
   173→          textAnchor="middle"
   174→          className="text-[8px] fill-white/15 font-mono"
   175→        >
   176→          lightness
   177→        </text>
   178→        <text

   180→          y={h - 4}
   181→          textAnchor="end"
   182→          className="text-[8px] fill-white/15 font-mono"
   183→        >
   184→          light
   185→        </text>
   186→      </svg>
   187→
   188→      {/* Formula row */}
   189→      <div className="px-4 py-2.5 border-t border-white/[0.06] bg-black/30 font-mono text-[11px] text-white/45 flex items-center justify-between gap-4 flex-wrap">
   190→        <div>
   191→          <span className="text-amber-400/60">S</span>(L) = 1 + ((L - 50)
   192→          <sup>2</sup> / <span className="text-amber-400/60">p</span> - 50
   193→          <sup>2</sup> / <span className="text-amber-400/60">p</span>) / 100

   196→          L = lightness &middot;{" "}
```

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-6 2026-03-13 09:46:16Z)**_



Yes, exactly that. HSL L10 is the same darkness either way, but on a white background the contrast makes it *appear* darker. On your dark modal background, the same swatch blends in and looks less dark by comparison.

The original range (10-90) is fine for the concept. The real question is whether you want to match the article's values for accuracy, or push the range wider so the strips read well against the dark background. Up to you.

```text
   100→
   101→const strips = buildStrips();
   102→
   103→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   104→// Curve + formula figure (compact, shown below the explanation)
   105→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   106→
   107→function CurveAndFormula() {
   108→  const w = 480;
   109→  const h = 140;
   110→  const pad = { l: 40, r: 12, t: 16, b: 24 };
   111→  const pw = w - pad.l - pad.r;
   112→  const ph = h - pad.t - pad.b;
   113→
   114→  const points: { x: number; y: number }[] = [];
   115→  for (let l = 0; l <= 100; l += 2) {
   116→    points.push({ x: l, y: getSaturation(l, REF_SMOD) });
   117→  }
   118→  const allY = points.map((p) => p.y);
   119→  const yMin = Math.min(...allY) * 0.92;
   120→  const yMax = Math.max(...allY) * 1.06;
   121→
   122→  const toX = (l: number) => pad.l + (l / 100) * pw;
   123→  const toY = (v: number) => pad.t + (1 - (v - yMin) / (yMax - yMin)) * ph;
   124→
   125→  const d = points
   126→    .map(
   127→      (p, i) =>
   128→        `${i === 0 ? "M" : "L"}${toX(p.x).toFixed(1)},${toY(p.y).toFixed(1)}`,
   129→    )
   130→    .join("");
   131→
   132→  const baselineY = toY(1);
   133→
   134→  return (
   135→    <div className="rounded-xl border border-white/[0.06] bg-white/[0.02] overflow-hidden">
   136→      <svg viewBox={`0 0 ${w} ${h}`} className="w-full h-auto">
   137→        {/* Baseline */}
   138→        <line
   139→          x1={pad.l}
   140→          y1={baselineY}
   141→          x2={w - pad.r}
   142→          y2={baselineY}
   143→          stroke="rgba(255,255,255,0.1)"
   144→          strokeWidth={1}
   145→          strokeDasharray="4 3"
   146→        />
   147→        <text
   148→          x={pad.l - 6}
   149→          y={baselineY + 3}
   150→          textAnchor="end"
   151→          className="text-[8px] fill-white/25 font-mono"
   152→        >
   153→          1.0x
   154→        </text>
   155→
   156→        {/* Fill under curve */}
   157→        <path
   158→          d={`${d} L${toX(100).toFixed(1)},${(pad.t + ph).toFixed(1)} L${toX(0).toFixed(1)},${(pad.t + ph).toFixed(1)} Z`}
   159→          fill="url(#aboutCurveFill)"
   160→        />
   161→        <defs>
   162→          <linearGradient id="aboutCurveFill" x1="0" y1="0" x2="0" y2="1">
   163→            <stop
   164→              offset="0%"
   165→              stopColor="hsl(38 90% 55%)"
   166→              stopOpacity="0.1"
   167→            />
   168→            <stop
   169→              offset="100%"
```
</details></tool-use>

Yeah it's confusing. The `SAT_CURVED` value (0.35) is the input saturation that gets multiplied by the curve — at the extremes the multiplier is ~1.0 so you get ~35%, at the midpoint the multiplier drops to ~0.375 so you get ~13%. So it's really the maximum, not the middle.

```text
   116→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   117→// Curve + formula figure (compact, shown below the explanation)
   118→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   119→
   120→function CurveAndFormula() {
   121→  const w = 480;
   122→  const h = 140;
   123→  const pad = { l: 40, r: 12, t: 16, b: 24 };
   124→  const pw = w - pad.l - pad.r;
   125→  const ph = h - pad.t - pad.b;
   126→
   127→  const points: { x: number; y: number }[] = [];
   128→  for (let l = 0; l <= 100; l += 2) {
   129→    points.push({ x: l, y: getSaturation(l, REF_SMOD) });
   130→  }
   131→  const allY = points.map((p) => p.y);
   132→  const yMin = Math.min(...allY) * 0.92;
   133→  const yMax = Math.max(...allY) * 1.06;
   134→
   135→  const toX = (l: number) => pad.l + (l / 100) * pw;
   136→  const toY = (v: number) => pad.t + (1 - (v - yMin) / (yMax - yMin)) * ph;
   137→
   138→  const d = points
   139→    .map(
   140→      (p, i) =>
   141→        `${i === 0 ? "M" : "L"}${toX(p.x).toFixed(1)},${toY(p.y).toFixed(1)}`,
   142→    )
   143→    .join("");
   144→
   145→  const baselineY = toY(1);
   146→
   147→  return (
   148→    <div className="rounded-xl border border-white/[0.06] bg-white/[0.02] overflow-hidden">
   149→      <svg viewBox={`0 0 ${w} ${h}`} className="w-full h-auto">
   150→        {/* Baseline */}
   151→        <line
   152→          x1={pad.l}
   153→          y1={baselineY}
   154→          x2={w - pad.r}
   155→          y2={baselineY}
   156→          stroke="rgba(255,255,255,0.1)"
   157→          strokeWidth={1}
   158→          strokeDasharray="4 3"
   159→        />
   160→        <text
   161→          x={pad.l - 6}
   162→          y={baselineY + 3}
   163→          textAnchor="end"
   164→          className="text-[8px] fill-white/25 font-mono"
   165→        >
   166→          1.0x
   167→        </text>
   168→
   169→        {/* Fill under curve */}
   170→        <path
   171→          d={`${d} L${toX(100).toFixed(1)},${(pad.t + ph).toFixed(1)} L${toX(0).toFixed(1)},${(pad.t + ph).toFixed(1)} Z`}
   172→          fill="url(#aboutCurveFill)"
   173→        />
   174→        <defs>
   175→          <linearGradient id="aboutCurveFill" x1="0" y1="0" x2="0" y2="1">
   176→            <stop
   177→              offset="0%"
   178→              stopColor="hsl(38 90% 55%)"
   179→              stopOpacity="0.1"
   180→            />
```

_**Agent - sidechain (2026-03-13 10:36:31Z)**_

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-6 2026-03-13 10:36:43Z)**_

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-6 2026-03-13 10:36:47Z)**_

You're right — I arbitrarily scaled it to 50% to match the article's visual instead of using our actual formula. That's dishonest to the viewer.

We have two honest options:

1. **Use our real curve** (`SAT_CURVED * getSaturation(l, sMod)`) — edges reach ~31%, which is accurate to what the tool does
2. **Frame it as the general perceptual boundary** — not "our" curve, but the underlying phenomenon. The article's author drew it by eye on Figma's picker. Our formula is an approximation of that boundary, not the boundary itself.

Option 2 is more faithful to the article's narrative (the picker reveals a natural curve, and we approximate it with a parabola). But then we should label it differently — something like "perceptual neutral boundary" rather than implying it's our formula.

Which direction do you want to go?

+// Color picker plane — saturation (x) vs lightness (y) with parabolic curve
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+function ColorPickerPlane() {
+  const size = 200;
+  const res = 40; // grid resolution
+  const cellSize = size / res;
+
+  // Build pixel grid
+  const cells: { x: number; y: number; color: string }[] = [];
+  for (let row = 0; row < res; row++) {
+    for (let col = 0; col < res; col++) {
+      const sat = col / (res - 1); // 0→1 left to right
+      const light = 1 - row / (res - 1); // 1→0 top to bottom
+      cells.push({
+        x: col * cellSize,
+        y: row * cellSize,
+        color: chroma.hsl(REF_HUE, sat, light).hex(),
+      });
+    }
+  }
+
+  // Parabolic curve: perceptual boundary scaled so edges reach ~50%
+  const curvePoints: string[] = [];
+  const edgeMultiplier = getSaturation(0, REF_SMOD);
+  const boundaryScale = 0.5 / edgeMultiplier; // normalize so edges = 50%
+  for (let l = 0; l <= 100; l += 2) {
+    const multiplier = getSaturation(l, REF_SMOD);
+    const sat = boundaryScale * multiplier;

```text
   129→function ColorPickerPlane() {
   130→  const size = 200;
   131→  const res = 40; // grid resolution
   132→  const cellSize = size / res;
   133→
   134→  // Build pixel grid
   135→  const cells: { x: number; y: number; color: string }[] = [];
   136→  for (let row = 0; row < res; row++) {
   137→    for (let col = 0; col < res; col++) {
   138→      const sat = col / (res - 1); // 0→1 left to right
   139→      const light = 1 - row / (res - 1); // 1→0 top to bottom
   140→      cells.push({
   141→        x: col * cellSize,
   142→        y: row * cellSize,
   143→        color: chroma.hsl(REF_HUE, sat, light).hex(),
   144→      });
   145→    }
   146→  }
   147→
   148→  // Parabolic curve: perceptual boundary scaled so edges reach ~50%
   149→  const curvePoints: string[] = [];
   150→  const edgeMultiplier = getSaturation(0, REF_SMOD);
   151→  const boundaryScale = 0.5 / edgeMultiplier; // normalize so edges = 50%
   152→  for (let l = 0; l <= 100; l += 2) {
   153→    const multiplier = getSaturation(l, REF_SMOD);
   154→    const sat = boundaryScale * multiplier;
   155→    const px = sat * size;
   156→    const py = (1 - l / 100) * size;
   157→    curvePoints.push(`${l === 0 ? "M" : "L"}${px.toFixed(1)},${py.toFixed(1)}`);
   158→  }
   159→
   160→  return (
   161→    <div className="flex gap-1.5">
   162→      <span className="text-[10px] font-mono text-white/25 self-center" style={{ writingMode: "vertical-rl", transform: "rotate(180deg)" }}>
   163→        Lightness
   164→      </span>
   165→      <div className="flex flex-col gap-1.5">
   166→        <div className="rounded-lg overflow-hidden border border-white/[0.06]">
   167→          <svg viewBox={`0 0 ${size} ${size}`} className="w-full h-auto block">
   168→            {cells.map((c, i) => (
   169→              <rect key={i} x={c.x} y={c.y} width={cellSize + 0.5} height={cellSize + 0.5} fill={c.color} />
   170→            ))}
   171→            <motion.path
   172→              d={curvePoints.join("")}
   173→              fill="none"
   174→              stroke="white"
   175→              strokeWidth={1.5}
   176→              strokeDasharray="4 3"
   177→              opacity={0.6}

   182→          </svg>
   183→        </div>
```

<summary>
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user is iteratively building and refining a "How it works" modal for a neutral color palette generator app. The modal explains the parabolic saturation curve concept through visual aids (color strips, a color picker plane, and a curve graph). The user wants the explanation to follow a clear problem→solution narrative, using accurate design terminology, proper attribution, and compelling visualizations. The current focus is on making the curve graph and color picker visuals work together to demonstrate that the mathematical formula matches the perceptual boundary visible in a color picker.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - Parabolic saturation curve: `getSaturation(lightness, sMod)` varies saturation multiplier across lightness
   - Formula: `1 + ((L - 50)^2 / p - 50^2 / p) / 100` where L=lightness, p=adjustment
   - Three-strip comparison: 0% (achromatic), 20% fixed (low), 40% fixed (high), boundary (50% edges), adjusted curve (31% edges)
   - Per-swatch indicators: `lo`/`ok`/`hi` text labels below strips
   - React/Vite/TypeScript, Tailwind v4, Zustand, Radix UI Dialog, Framer Motion
   - chroma-js for color manipulation
   - SVG color picker plane with animated parabolic curve overlay (clip-path wipe + staggered swatch outlines)
   - BOUNDARY_SCALE normalizes the curve so strip edges reach 50% saturation

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - `/Users/grant/code/chroma-colours-neutrals/app/src/components/About.tsx`
     - The primary file being edited throughout. Contains all modal components.
     - Key constants:
     ```typescript
     const REF_HUE = 220;
     ```

```text
    11→// Saturation tuned for the midrange — extremes lose chroma
    12→const SAT_FOR_MID = 0.20;
    13→// Saturation tuned for the extremes — midtones are over-saturated
    14→const SAT_FOR_EXTREMES = 0.40;
    15→// Curve-modulated: base sat that produces balanced results
    16→const SAT_CURVED = 0.35;
    17→
    18→// Swatch classification for 9 steps (indices 0-8)
    19→// "low" = not intense enough, "ok" = desired, "high" = too intense
    20→type SwatchMark = "low" | "ok" | "high" | null;
    21→
    22→// 20% strip: extremes not intense, center too intense, transition zones correct
    23→const markForMid = (i: number): SwatchMark => {
    24→  if (i <= 1 || i >= 7) return "low";
    25→  if (i === 4) return "high";
    26→  return "ok";
    27→};
    28→
    29→// 40% strip: extremes correct, midtones too intense
    30→const markForExtremes = (i: number): SwatchMark => {
    31→  if (i <= 1 || i >= 7) return "ok";
    32→  return "high";
    33→};
    34→
    35→// Curved: all correct
    36→const markCurved = (): SwatchMark => "ok";
    37→
    38→function buildStrips() {
    39→  const zero: string[] = [];
    40→  const forMid: string[] = [];
    41→  const forExtremes: string[] = [];
    42→  const boundary: string[] = [];
    43→  const curved: string[] = [];
    44→  for (let i = 0; i < STRIP_STEPS; i++) {
    45→    const lInt = 5 + i * (90 / (STRIP_STEPS - 1)); // L5 through L95
    46→    const l = lInt / 100;
    47→    zero.push(chroma.hsl(REF_HUE, 0, l).hex());
    48→    forMid.push(chroma.hsl(REF_HUE, SAT_FOR_MID, l).hex());
    49→    forExtremes.push(chroma.hsl(REF_HUE, SAT_FOR_EXTREMES, l).hex());
    50→    boundary.push(
    51→      chroma
    52→        .hsl(REF_HUE, BOUNDARY_SCALE * getSaturation(lInt, REF_SMOD), l)
    53→        .hex(),
    54→    );
    55→    curved.push(
    56→      chroma
    57→        .hsl(REF_HUE, SAT_CURVED * getSaturation(lInt, REF_SMOD), l)
    58→        .hex(),
    59→    );
    60→  }
    61→  return { zero, forMid, forExtremes, boundary, curved };
    62→}
    63→
    64→// Lightness step labels
    65→const LIGHTNESS_LABELS = Array.from({ length: STRIP_STEPS }, (_, i) =>
    66→  Math.round(5 + i * (90 / (STRIP_STEPS - 1)))
    67→);
    68→
    69→// Scale factor so parabola edges reach 50% sat (perceptual boundary)
    70→const BOUNDARY_SCALE = 0.5 / getSaturation(LIGHTNESS_LABELS[0], REF_SMOD);
    71→
    72→interface StripProps {
    73→  colors: string[];
    74→  label?: string;
    75→  mark?: (index: number) => SwatchMark;
    76→}
    77→
    78→function Strip({ colors, label, mark }: StripProps) {
    79→  return (
    80→    <div>
    81→      {label && (
    82→        <span className="text-[9px] font-mono uppercase tracking-wider text-white/20 mb-1 block">{label}</span>
    83→      )}
    84→      <div className="flex rounded-lg overflow-hidden">
    85→        {colors.map((c, i) => (
    86→          <div key={i} className="flex-1 h-12" style={{ backgroundColor: c }} />
    87→        ))}
    88→      </div>
    89→      {mark && (
    90→        <div className="flex mt-1">
    91→          {colors.map((_, i) => {
    92→            const m = mark(i);
    93→            const text =
    94→              m === "ok" ? "ok" : m === "high" ? "hi" : m === "low" ? "lo" : null;
    95→            return (
    96→              <div key={i} className="flex-1 text-center leading-1.5">
    97→                {text && (
    98→                  <span className={`text-[8px] font-mono ${m === "ok" ? "text-white/75" : "text-white/25"}`}>
    99→                    {text}
   100→                  </span>
   101→                )}
   102→              </div>
   103→            );
   104→          })}
   105→        </div>
   106→      )}
   107→    </div>
   108→  );
   109→}
   110→
   111→function LightnessHeader() {
   112→  return (
   113→    <div className="flex">
   114→      {LIGHTNESS_LABELS.map((l) => (
   115→        <div key={l} className="flex-1 text-center text-[9px] font-mono text-white/20">
   116→          {l}
   117→        </div>
   118→      ))}
   119→    </div>
   120→  );
   121→}
   122→
   123→const strips = buildStrips();
   124→
   125→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   126→// Color picker plane — saturation (x) vs lightness (y) with parabolic curve
   127→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   128→
   129→function ColorPickerPlane() {
   130→  const size = 200;
   131→  const res = 40; // grid resolution
   132→  const cellSize = size / res;
   133→
   134→  // Build pixel grid
   135→  const cells: { x: number; y: number; color: string }[] = [];
   136→  for (let row = 0; row < res; row++) {
   137→    for (let col = 0; col < res; col++) {
   138→      const sat = col / (res - 1); // 0→1 left to right
   139→      const light = 1 - row / (res - 1); // 1→0 top to bottom
   140→      cells.push({
   141→        x: col * cellSize,
   142→        y: row * cellSize,
   143→        color: chroma.hsl(REF_HUE, sat, light).hex(),
   144→      });
   145→    }
   146→  }
   147→
   148→  // Parabolic curve: perceptual boundary scaled so edges reach ~50%
   149→  // Path goes top (l=100) to bottom (l=0) so it draws top→bottom
   150→  const curvePoints: string[] = [];
   151→  const edgeMultiplier = getSaturation(0, REF_SMOD);
   152→  const boundaryScale = 0.5 / edgeMultiplier; // normalize so edges = 50%
   153→  for (let l = 100; l >= 0; l -= 2) {
   154→    const multiplier = getSaturation(l, REF_SMOD);
   155→    const sat = boundaryScale * multiplier;
   156→    const px = sat * size;
   157→    const py = (1 - l / 100) * size;
   158→    curvePoints.push(`${l === 100 ? "M" : "L"}${px.toFixed(1)},${py.toFixed(1)}`);
   159→  }
   160→
   161→  // Swatches along the curve (one per grid row, sampled every few rows)
   162→  const sampleInterval = 4;
   163→  const curveDots: { cx: number; cy: number; delay: number }[] = [];
   164→  for (let row = 0; row < res; row += sampleInterval) {
   165→    const light = 1 - row / (res - 1);
   166→    const lInt = Math.round(light * 100);
   167→    const multiplier = getSaturation(lInt, REF_SMOD);
   168→    const sat = boundaryScale * multiplier;
   169→    curveDots.push({
   170→      cx: sat * size,
   171→      cy: row * cellSize,
   172→      delay: 0.5 + (row / res) * 1.2,
   173→    });
   174→  }
   175→
   176→  // Boundary cells: for each row, find the column on the curve
   177→  // Timeline (12s cycle):
   178→  //   0-2s   bare picker
   179→  //   2-4s   line draws top→bottom
   180→  //   4-5s   line holds
   181→  //   5-6s   line fades out
   182→  //   6-8s   swatches highlight sequentially top→bottom
   183→  //   8-9s   swatches hold
   184→  //   9-10s  swatches fade
   185→  //   10-12s bare picker
   186→  const pause = 1;
   187→  const drawDur = 2;
   188→  const lineHold = 2;
   189→  const lineFade = 1;
   190→  const swatchStart = pause + drawDur + lineHold + lineFade; // 6s
   191→  const swatchDraw = 2;
   192→  const swatchHold = 2;
   193→  const swatchFade = 1;
   194→  const cycle = 12;
   195→
   196→  const boundaryCells: { x: number; y: number; stagger: number }[] = [];
   197→  for (let row = 0; row < res; row++) {
   198→    const light = 1 - row / (res - 1);
   199→    const lInt = Math.round(light * 100);
   200→    const multiplier = getSaturation(lInt, REF_SMOD);
   201→    const sat = boundaryScale * multiplier;
   202→    const col = Math.round(sat * (res - 1));
   203→    if (col >= 0 && col < res) {
   204→      boundaryCells.push({
   205→        x: col * cellSize,
   206→        y: row * cellSize,
   207→        stagger: (row / res) * swatchDraw, // offset within the swatch phase
   208→      });
   209→    }
   210→  }
   211→
   212→  return (
   213→    <div className="flex gap-1.5">
   214→      <span className="text-[10px] font-mono text-white/25 self-center" style={{ writingMode: "vertical-rl", transform: "rotate(180deg)" }}>
   215→        Lightness
   216→      </span>
   217→      <div className="flex flex-col gap-1.5">
   218→        <div className="rounded-lg overflow-hidden border border-white/[0.06]">
   219→          <svg viewBox={`0 0 ${size} ${size}`} className="w-full h-auto block">
   220→            {cells.map((c, i) => (
   221→              <rect key={i} x={c.x} y={c.y} width={cellSize + 0.5} height={cellSize + 0.5} fill={c.color} />
   222→            ))}
   223→            {/* Clip mask for line: wipes top→bottom in, then top→bottom out */}
   224→            <defs>
   225→              <clipPath id="lineReveal">
   226→                <motion.rect
   227→                  x={0}
   228→                  width={size}
   229→                  initial={{ y: 0, height: 0 }}
   230→                  animate={{
   231→                    y:      [0, 0, 0,    0,    size],
   232→                    height: [0, 0, size,  size, 0],
   233→                  }}
   234→                  transition={{
   235→                    duration: cycle,
   236→                    times: [
   237→                      0,
   238→                      pause / cycle,                                       // 2s: start draw
   239→                      (pause + drawDur) / cycle,                           // 4s: fully revealed
   240→                      (pause + drawDur + lineHold) / cycle,                // 5s: start exit
   241→                      (pause + drawDur + lineHold + lineFade) / cycle,     // 6s: fully hidden
   242→                    ],
   243→                    ease: "easeInOut",
   244→                    repeat: Infinity,
   245→                  }}
   246→                />
   247→              </clipPath>
   248→            </defs>
   249→            {/* Dashed curve line */}
   250→            <path
   251→              d={curvePoints.join("")}
   252→              fill="none"
   253→              stroke="white"
   254→              strokeWidth={1.5}
   255→              strokeDasharray="4 3"
   256→              opacity={0.6}
   257→              clipPath="url(#lineReveal)"
   258→            />
   259→            {/* Outline swatches: appear sequentially, then disappear sequentially */}
   260→            {boundaryCells.map((cell, i) => {
   261→              const onTime = (swatchStart + cell.stagger) / cycle;
   262→              const onEnd = Math.min(onTime + 0.01, 1);
   263→              const offStart = (swatchStart + swatchDraw + swatchHold + cell.stagger * (swatchFade / swatchDraw)) / cycle;
   264→              const offEnd = Math.min(offStart + 0.01, 1);
   265→              return (
   266→                <motion.rect
   267→                  key={`b-${i}`}
   268→                  x={cell.x}
   269→                  y={cell.y}
   270→                  width={cellSize}
   271→                  height={cellSize}
   272→                  fill="none"
   273→                  stroke="white"
   274→                  strokeWidth={1}
   275→                  initial={{ opacity: 0 }}
   276→                  animate={{
   277→                    opacity: [0, 0, 0.9, 0.9, 0, 0],
   278→                  }}
   279→                  transition={{
   280→                    duration: cycle,
   281→                    times: [0, onTime, onEnd, offStart, offEnd, 1],
   282→                    ease: "linear",
   283→                    repeat: Infinity,
   284→                  }}
   285→                />
   286→              );
   287→            })}
   288→          </svg>
   289→        </div>
   290→        <span className="text-[10px] font-mono text-white/25 text-center">
   291→          Saturation
   292→        </span>
   293→      </div>
   294→    </div>
   295→  );
   296→}
   297→
   298→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   299→// Curve + formula figure
   300→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   301→
   302→function CurveAndFormula() {
   303→  const w = 320;
   304→  const h = 120;
   305→  const pad = { l: 32, r: 10, t: 12, b: 22 };
   306→  const pw = w - pad.l - pad.r;
   307→  const ph = h - pad.t - pad.b;
   308→
   309→  const points: { x: number; y: number }[] = [];
   310→  for (let l = 0; l <= 100; l += 2) {
   311→    points.push({ x: l, y: getSaturation(l, REF_SMOD) });
   312→  }
   313→  const allY = points.map((p) => p.y);
   314→  const yMin = Math.min(...allY) * 0.92;
   315→  const yMax = Math.max(...allY) * 1.06;
   316→
   317→  const toX = (l: number) => pad.l + (l / 100) * pw;
   318→  const toY = (v: number) => pad.t + (1 - (v - yMin) / (yMax - yMin)) * ph;
   319→
   320→  const d = points
   321→    .map(
   322→      (p, i) =>
   323→        `${i === 0 ? "M" : "L"}${toX(p.x).toFixed(1)},${toY(p.y).toFixed(1)}`,
   324→    )
   325→    .join("");
   326→
   327→  const baselineY = toY(1);
   328→
   329→  return (
   330→    <div className="rounded-xl border border-white/[0.06] bg-white/[0.02] overflow-hidden">
   331→      <svg viewBox={`0 0 ${w} ${h}`} className="w-full h-auto">
   332→        {/* Baseline */}
   333→        <line
   334→          x1={pad.l}
   335→          y1={baselineY}
   336→          x2={w - pad.r}
   337→          y2={baselineY}
   338→          stroke="rgba(255,255,255,0.1)"
   339→          strokeWidth={1}
   340→          strokeDasharray="4 3"
   341→        />
   342→        <text
   343→          x={pad.l - 6}
   344→          y={baselineY + 3}
   345→          textAnchor="end"
   346→          className="text-[8px] fill-white/25 font-mono"
   347→        >
   348→          1.0x
   349→        </text>
   350→
   351→        {/* Fill under curve */}
   352→        <path
   353→          d={`${d} L${toX(100).toFixed(1)},${(pad.t + ph).toFixed(1)} L${toX(0).toFixed(1)},${(pad.t + ph).toFixed(1)} Z`}
   354→          fill="url(#aboutCurveFill)"
   355→        />
   356→        <defs>
   357→          <linearGradient id="aboutCurveFill" x1="0" y1="0" x2="0" y2="1">
   358→            <stop
   359→              offset="0%"
   360→              stopColor="hsl(38 90% 55%)"
   361→              stopOpacity="0.1"
   362→            />
   363→            <stop
   364→              offset="100%"
   365→              stopColor="hsl(38 90% 55%)"
   366→              stopOpacity="0.01"
   367→            />
   368→          </linearGradient>
   369→        </defs>
   370→
   371→        {/* Curve */}
   372→        <path
   373→          d={d}
   374→          fill="none"
   375→          stroke="hsl(38 90% 55%)"
   376→          strokeWidth={2}
   377→          strokeLinecap="round"
   378→          strokeLinejoin="round"
   379→        />
   380→
   381→        {/* Annotations */}
   382→        <text
   383→          x={toX(50)}
   384→          y={toY(getSaturation(50, REF_SMOD)) + 14}
   385→          textAnchor="middle"
   386→          className="text-[9px] fill-white/25 font-mono"
   387→        >
   388→          min at L=50
   389→        </text>
   390→
   391→        {/* X axis */}
   392→        <text
   393→          x={toX(0)}
   394→          y={h - 4}
   395→          className="text-[8px] fill-white/15 font-mono"
   396→        >
   397→          dark
   398→        </text>
   399→        <text
   400→          x={toX(50)}
   401→          y={h - 4}
   402→          textAnchor="middle"
   403→          className="text-[8px] fill-white/15 font-mono"
   404→        >
   405→          lightness
   406→        </text>
   407→        <text
   408→          x={toX(100)}
   409→          y={h - 4}
   410→          textAnchor="end"
   411→          className="text-[8px] fill-white/15 font-mono"
   412→        >
   413→          light
   414→        </text>
   415→      </svg>
   416→
   417→    </div>
   418→  );
   419→}
   420→
   421→function Formula() {
   422→  return (
   423→    <div className="rounded-xl border border-white/[0.06] bg-white/[0.02] px-4 py-2.5 font-mono text-[11px] text-white/45 flex items-center justify-between gap-4 flex-wrap">
   424→      <div>
   425→        <span className="text-amber-400/60">S</span>(L) = 1 + ((L - 50)
   426→        <sup>2</sup> / <span className="text-amber-400/60">p</span> - 50
   427→        <sup>2</sup> / <span className="text-amber-400/60">p</span>) / 100
   428→      </div>
   429→      <div className="text-[10px] text-white/25">
   430→        L = lightness &middot;{" "}
   431→        <span className="text-amber-400/40">p</span> = adjustment
   432→      </div>
   433→    </div>
   434→  );
   435→}
   436→
   437→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   438→// Exported components
   439→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   440→
   441→export function AboutButton() {
   442→  const [open, setOpen] = useState(false);
   443→
   444→  return (
   445→    <Dialog.Root open={open} onOpenChange={setOpen}>
   446→      <Dialog.Trigger asChild>
   447→        <button
   448→          type="button"
   449→          className="w-8 h-8 flex items-center justify-center rounded-lg text-white/40 hover:text-white/70 hover:bg-white/[0.06] transition-colors cursor-pointer"
   450→          aria-label="About this tool"
   451→        >
   452→          <svg width="16" height="16" viewBox="0 0 16 16" fill="none">
   453→            <circle
   454→              cx="8"
   455→              cy="8"
   456→              r="7"
   457→              stroke="currentColor"
   458→              strokeWidth="1.5"
   459→            />
   460→            <path
   461→              d="M8 7v4.5M8 4.5v.01"
   462→              stroke="currentColor"
   463→              strokeWidth="1.5"
   464→              strokeLinecap="round"
   465→            />
   466→          </svg>
   467→        </button>
   468→      </Dialog.Trigger>
   469→      <AnimatePresence>
   470→        {open && (
   471→          <Dialog.Portal forceMount>
   472→            <Dialog.Overlay asChild>
   473→              <motion.div
   474→                initial={{ opacity: 0 }}
   475→                animate={{ opacity: 1 }}
   476→                exit={{ opacity: 0 }}
   477→                transition={{ duration: 0.15 }}
   478→                className="fixed inset-0 bg-black/60 backdrop-blur-sm z-50"
   479→              />
   480→            </Dialog.Overlay>
   481→            <Dialog.Content asChild>
   482→              <motion.div
   483→                initial={{ opacity: 0, scale: 0.96, y: 8 }}
   484→                animate={{ opacity: 1, scale: 1, y: 0 }}
   485→                exit={{ opacity: 0, scale: 0.96, y: 8 }}
   486→                transition={{ duration: 0.2 }}
   487→                className="fixed left-1/2 top-1/2 -translate-x-1/2 -translate-y-1/2 z-50 w-full max-w-3xl p-6 rounded-2xl bg-[hsl(220_10%_6%)] border border-white/[0.08] shadow-2xl max-h-[85vh] overflow-y-auto"
   488→              >
   489→                <Dialog.Title className="text-lg font-semibold text-white/90 mb-2">
   490→                  How it works
   491→                </Dialog.Title>
   492→
   493→                <p className="text-[13px] leading-relaxed text-white/50 mb-8">
   494→                  Neutral palettes need saturation that varies with lightness.
   495→                  A fixed saturation value always compromises one end of the
   496→                  scale.
   497→                </p>
   498→
   499→                <div className="space-y-8">
   500→                  {/* Table of strips */}
   501→                  <div className="grid grid-cols-[1fr_1.4fr] gap-x-6 gap-y-5 items-center">
   502→                    {/* Header row */}
   503→                    <div />
   504→                    <div>
   505→                      <div className="flex items-center gap-1 mb-1">
   506→                        <span className="text-[9px] font-mono text-white/25 uppercase tracking-wider">Lightness</span>
   507→                      </div>
   508→                      <LightnessHeader />
   509→                    </div>
   510→
   511→                    {/* 0% sat */}
   512→                    <div>
   513→                      <div className="flex items-baseline justify-between mb-1">
   514→                        <p className="text-[13px] font-semibold text-white/70">Achromatic Baseline</p>
   515→                        <span className="text-[10px] font-mono text-white/25">Saturation: 0%</span>
   516→                      </div>
   517→                      <p className="text-[13px] leading-relaxed text-white/45">
   518→                        Unsaturated grayscale. Functional, but lacks character or brand identity.
   519→                      </p>
   520→                    </div>
   521→                    <Strip colors={strips.zero} />
   522→
   523→                    {/* 20% sat */}
   524→                    <div>
   525→                      <div className="flex items-baseline justify-between mb-1">
   526→                        <p className="text-[13px] font-semibold text-white/70">Fixed low saturation</p>
   527→                        <span className="text-[10px] font-mono text-white/25">Saturation: {Math.round(SAT_FOR_MID * 100)}%</span>
   528→                      </div>
   529→                      <p className="text-[13px] leading-relaxed text-white/45">
   530→                        Desired neutral midtones, but dark and light ends become effectively desaturated.
   531→                      </p>
   532→                    </div>
   533→                    <Strip colors={strips.forMid} mark={markForMid} />
   534→
   535→                    {/* 40% sat */}
   536→                    <div>
   537→                      <div className="flex items-baseline justify-between mb-1">
   538→                        <p className="text-[13px] font-semibold text-white/70">Fixed high saturation</p>
   539→                        <span className="text-[10px] font-mono text-white/25">Saturation: {Math.round(SAT_FOR_EXTREMES * 100)}%</span>
   540→                      </div>
   541→                      <p className="text-[13px] leading-relaxed text-white/45">
   542→                        Raising it to recover the extremes pushes the midrange
   543→                        to be oversaturated.
   544→                      </p>
   545→                    </div>
   546→                    <Strip colors={strips.forExtremes} mark={markForExtremes} />
   547→
   548→                  </div>
   549→
   550→                  {/* The solution */}
   551→                  <div className="mt-2 pt-8 border-t border-white/[0.06]">
   552→                    <p className="text-[13px] font-semibold text-white/70 mb-3">The solution</p>
   553→
   554→                    <div className="grid grid-cols-[1fr_1fr] gap-6 items-start">
   555→                      <div>
   556→                        <p className="text-[13px] leading-relaxed text-white/45 mb-3">
   557→                          In a color picker, the boundary between neutral and
   558→                          chromatic is not a straight line. It follows a curve:
   559→                          colours at the extremes of lightness need more
   560→                          saturation to register as tinted, while midtones
   561→                          need less.
   562→                          <sup className="text-[9px] text-white/25 ml-0.5">1</sup>
   563→                        </p>
```

- **Axis labels** (on pickers, graphs): `text-[10px] font-mono text-[hsl(220_10%_50%)]`, title case

- **Lightness header**: match strip label style
- **Saturation values** next to strip titles: `text-[10px] font-mono text-[hsl(220_10%_50%)]`

```text
   655→                          {"Saturation: "}

   661→                        </span>
   662→                      </div>
   663→                      <p className="text-[13px] leading-relaxed text-white/45">
   664→                        Sampling saturation along the curve produces even
   665→                        perceived chroma, but is too saturated for neutrals.
   666→                      </p>
   667→                    </div>

   669→                  </div>

   677→                          {"Saturation: "}

   683→                        </span>
   684→                      </div>
   685→                      <p className="text-[13px] leading-relaxed text-white/45">

   692→                  </div>
```

```text
    15→// Curve-modulated: base sat that produces balanced results
    16→const SAT_CURVED = 0.35;
    17→
    18→// Swatch classification for 9 steps (indices 0-8)
    19→// "low" = not intense enough, "ok" = desired, "high" = too intense
    20→type SwatchMark = "low" | "ok" | "high" | null;
    21→
    22→// 20% strip: extremes not intense, center too intense, transition zones correct
    23→const markForMid = (i: number): SwatchMark => {
    24→  if (i <= 1 || i >= 7) return "low";
    25→  if (i === 4) return "high";
    26→  return "ok";
    27→};
    28→
    29→// 40% strip: extremes correct, midtones too intense
    30→const markForExtremes = (i: number): SwatchMark => {
    31→  if (i <= 1 || i >= 7) return "ok";
    32→  return "high";
    33→};
    34→
    35→// Curved: all correct
    36→const markCurved = (): SwatchMark => "ok";
    37→
    38→function buildStrips() {
    39→  const zero: string[] = [];
    40→  const forMid: string[] = [];
    41→  const forExtremes: string[] = [];
    42→  const boundary: string[] = [];
    43→  const curved: string[] = [];
    44→  for (let i = 0; i < STRIP_STEPS; i++) {
    45→    const lInt = 5 + i * (90 / (STRIP_STEPS - 1)); // L5 through L95
    46→    const l = lInt / 100;
    47→    zero.push(chroma.hsl(REF_HUE, 0, l).hex());
    48→    forMid.push(chroma.hsl(REF_HUE, SAT_FOR_MID, l).hex());
    49→    forExtremes.push(chroma.hsl(REF_HUE, SAT_FOR_EXTREMES, l).hex());
    50→    boundary.push(
    51→      chroma
    52→        .hsl(REF_HUE, BOUNDARY_SCALE * getSaturation(lInt, REF_SMOD), l)
    53→        .hex(),
    54→    );
    55→    curved.push(
    56→      chroma
    57→        .hsl(REF_HUE, SAT_CURVED * getSaturation(lInt, REF_SMOD), l)
    58→        .hex(),
    59→    );
    60→  }
    61→  return { zero, forMid, forExtremes, boundary, curved };
    62→}
    63→
    64→// Lightness step labels
    65→const LIGHTNESS_LABELS = Array.from({ length: STRIP_STEPS }, (_, i) =>
    66→  Math.round(5 + i * (90 / (STRIP_STEPS - 1)))
    67→);
    68→
    69→// Scale factor so parabola edges reach 50% sat (perceptual boundary)
    70→const BOUNDARY_SCALE = 0.5 / getSaturation(LIGHTNESS_LABELS[0], REF_SMOD);
```
</details></tool-use>

<analysis>
Let me chronologically analyze the conversation from the context restoration point:

1. The conversation was restored from a previous session that was building an "About/How it works" modal for a neutral color palette generator app.

2. The first task was making the picker swatch highlight animation borders more subtle - changed strokeWidth from 1 to 0.5 and opacity from 0.9 to 0.35.

3. User wanted to try making the curve graph square and placing a rotated color picker next to it to show alignment between the mathematical formula and the perceptual boundary.

4. Created `CurvePickerAlignment` component with a square curve graph and rotated color picker side-by-side, sharing the same coordinate system. The rotated picker has lightness on x-axis and saturation on y-axis.

5. User said the pickers needed to be the same size - changed maxSat from 0.6 to 1.0 to show full saturation range.

6. User said they were NOT the same size (first SVG 257x257, second 264x264) - the difference was due to different gap and label sizes in the wrapper. Fixed by matching gap-1.5 and text-[10px] on both.

7. User asked to make the calc graph also the same size - added max-w-[280px] to the curve graph too.

8. User asked to reduce border radius on pickers and calc graph - changed rounded-lg to rounded.

9. User suggested using CSS grid for the solution section - refactored CurvePickerAlignment to return an object `{curveGraph, rotatedPicker}` via `useCurvePickerVisuals()`, created `SolutionSection` component with a single grid, and placed all three visuals in the same grid columns.

<summary>
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user is iteratively building and refining a "How it works" modal for a neutral color palette generator app. The modal explains a parabolic saturation curve concept through visual aids (color strips, animated color picker, curve graph, and rotated color picker). The conversation covered: adding a curve-picker alignment visualization, unifying the modal layout with CSS grid, standardizing text styles, adding a reference hue swatch with contextual labels, and most recently making the reference hue adjustable via a clickable swatch with a hue picker UI.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - Parabolic saturation curve: `getSaturation(lightness, sMod)` varies saturation multiplier across lightness
   - Formula: `1 + ((L - 50)^2 / p - 50^2 / p) / 100`
   - React/Vite/TypeScript, Tailwind v4, Radix UI Dialog, Framer Motion, chroma-js
   - CSS Grid used throughout the modal for consistent column sizing (`grid-cols-[1fr_1.4fr]` master grid)
   - SVG-based visualizations: animated color picker plane, curve graph, rotated color picker
   - Pointer events for hue picker drag interaction
   - `useMemo` for expensive color computation
   - Absolute positioning for labels flanking the hue swatch
   - Solid HSL colors instead of white/opacity for text (user feedback: never use opacity for text lightness)

3. Files and Code Sections:

   - `/Users/grant/code/chroma-colours-neutrals/app/src/components/About.tsx` — The primary file being edited throughout. Contains all modal components.
   
     **Key constants (being refactored to stateful):**
     ```typescript
     const DEFAULT_HUE = 220; // was REF_HUE, now used as initial state
     const REF_SMOD = 40;
     const STRIP_STEPS = 9;
     const SAT_FOR_MID = 0.20;
     const SAT_FOR_EXTREMES = 0.40;
     const SAT_CURVED = 0.35;
     const LIGHTNESS_LABELS = Array.from({ length: STRIP_STEPS }, (_, i) =>
       Math.round(5 + i * (90 / (STRIP_STEPS - 1)))
     );
     const BOUNDARY_SCALE = 0.5 / getSaturation(LIGHTNESS_LABELS[0], REF_SMOD);
     ```

     **`buildStrips` — now parameterized with hue:**
     ```typescript
     function buildStrips(hue: number) {
       // generates zero, forMid, forExtremes, boundary, curved color arrays
     }
     ```

     **`ColorPickerPlane` — animated picker with boundary line and swatch highlights:**
     ```typescript
     function ColorPickerPlane({ hue }: { hue: number }) {
       // 200x200 SVG grid, sat on x, lightness on y
       // Animated boundary line via clip-path wipe
       // Swatch outlines with staggered animation (strokeWidth 0.5, opacity 0.35)
       // 12s animation cycle
     }
     ```

     **`useCurvePickerVisuals` — returns curve graph and rotated picker JSX:**
     ```typescript
     function useCurvePickerVisuals(hue: number) {
       // Returns { curveGraph, rotatedPicker }
       // Both share same coordinate mapping (size=200, maxSat=1.0)
       // Curve graph: S(L) y-axis, 0/L/100 x-axis, gridlines at 10s
       // Rotated picker: lightness on x, saturation on y
       // VisualPanel wrapper ensures consistent axis label sizing
     }
     ```

     **`SolutionSection` — single CSS grid for text + picker + formula + alignment:**
     ```typescript
     function SolutionSection({ hue }: { hue: number }) {
       const { curveGraph, rotatedPicker } = useCurvePickerVisuals(hue);
       // grid-cols-[1fr_1fr] with rows: text|picker, formula(col-span-2), graph|rotated, caption
     }
     ```

     **`HuePicker` — newly created horizontal hue gradient bar:**
     ```typescript
     function HuePicker({ hue, onChange }: { hue: number; onChange: (h: number) => void }) {
       const barRef = useRef<HTMLDivElement>(null);
       const hueFromPointer = useCallback((clientX: number) => {
         const bar = barRef.current;
         if (!bar) return;
         const rect = bar.getBoundingClientRect();
         const ratio = Math.max(0, Math.min(1, (clientX - rect.left) / rect.width));
         onChange(Math.round(ratio * 360));
       }, [onChange]);
       // Renders gradient bar with thumb indicator, supports click and drag
     }
     ```

     **`AboutButton` — now holds hue state:**
     ```typescript
     export function AboutButton() {
       const [open, setOpen] = useState(false);
       const [hue, setHue] = useState(DEFAULT_HUE);
       const [showHuePicker, setShowHuePicker] = useState(false);
       const strips = useMemo(() => buildStrips(hue), [hue]);
     ```

     **Modal master grid layout:**
     ```tsx
     <div className="grid grid-cols-[1fr_1.4fr] gap-x-6 gap-y-8">
       {/* Title + intro — col-span-2 */}
       {/* Hue swatch — aligned with L=50 in 9-col subgrid, clickable */}
       {/* Lightness header */}
       {/* 0% / 20% / 40% sat strips */}
       {/* SolutionSection — col-span-2 */}
       {/* Boundary strip */}
       {/* Adjusted curve strip */}
     </div>
     ```

     **Hue swatch area with labels and picker:**
     ```tsx
     <div>
       <div className="relative">
         <div className="grid" style={{ gridTemplateColumns: `repeat(${STRIP_STEPS}, 1fr)` }}>
           {/* Only index 4 has the colored swatch, clickable */}
         </div>
         {/* "Reference hue for examples below" — absolute, right-aligned to swatch left */}
         {/* H/S/L values — absolute, left-aligned to swatch right, H at 13px, S/L dimmed */}
       </div>
       {showHuePicker && <HuePicker hue={hue} onChange={setHue} />}
     </div>
     ```

     **Text style convention established:**
     - All label text uses `text-[hsl(220_10%_50%)]` (solid color, no opacity)
     - Axis labels: 10px font-mono
     - Strip labels: 9px font-mono uppercase tracking-wider
     - Swatch indicators (lo/ok/hi): 8px, "ok" at `hsl(220_10%_65%)`, "lo/hi" at `hsl(220_10%_40%)`
     - Dimmed values (fixed S/L): `text-[hsl(220_10%_35%)]`

   - `/Users/grant/code/chroma-colours-neutrals/app/src/lib/palette.ts` — Core algorithm (unchanged in this session)

Initially tried `max-w-[280px]` on all three. Eventually solved by using a single CSS grid (`grid-cols-[1fr_1fr]`) for the solution section so all visuals share the same column width.
   - **Text labels wrapping in grid cells**: When placing "Hue: 220°" and "S: 100% L: 50%" in the 9-column swatch grid, they wrapped because cells were too narrow. Fixed by using absolute positioning with `whitespace-nowrap`.
   - **User feedback on opacity for text**: User explicitly said "i'd prefer if you didnt use opacity to manipulate the lightness especially for text, use actual values". Changed all `text-white/XX` label colors to `text-[hsl(220_10%_50%)]`.
   - **Contrast too low at 35%**: User said axis labels hard to read. Bumped from `hsl(220_10%_35%)` to `hsl(220_10%_50%)`.
   - **Text style inconsistencies**: Mix of sizes (8px, 9px, 10px), colors (white/20, white/25, hsl values), and capitalization (uppercase vs title case). Standardized throughout.

+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+// Hue picker — horizontal gradient bar for selecting hue angle
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+const HUE_PRESETS: { label: string; hue: number }[] = [
+  { label: "Red", hue: 0 },
+  { label: "Orange", hue: 30 },
+  { label: "Gold", hue: 45 },
+  { label: "Green", hue: 150 },
+  { label: "Teal", hue: 180 },
+  { label: "Blue", hue: 220 },
+  { label: "Indigo", hue: 245 },
+  { label: "Purple", hue: 280 },
+  { label: "Rose", hue: 340 },
+];
+
+function HuePicker({
+  hue,
+  onChange,
+  onReset,
+  isDefault,
+}: {
+  hue: number;
+  onChange: (h: number) => void;
+  onReset: () => void;
+  isDefault: boolean;
+}) {
+  const barRef = useRef<HTMLDivElement>(null);
+
+  const hueFromPointer = useCallback((clientX: number) => {
+    const bar = barRef.current;
+    if (!bar) return;
+    const rect = bar.getBoundingClientRect();
+    const ratio = Math.max(0, Math.min(1, (clientX - rect.left) / rect.width));
+    onChange(Math.round(ratio * 360));
+  }, [onChange]);
+
+  const onPointerDown = useCallback((e: React.PointerEvent) => {
+    e.currentTarget.setPointerCapture(e.pointerId);
+    hueFromPointer(e.clientX);
+  }, [hueFromPointer]);
+
+  const onPointerMove = useCallback((e: React.PointerEvent) => {
+    if (e.buttons === 0) return;
+    hueFromPointer(e.clientX);
+  }, [hueFromPointer]);
+
+  return (
+    <div className="space-y-2">
+      {/* Presets + reset */}
+      <div className="flex items-center gap-1.5">
+        {HUE_PRESETS.map((p) => (
+          <button
+            key={p.hue}
+            onClick={() => onChange(p.hue)}
+            title={`${p.label} (${p.hue}°)`}
+            className="h-5 flex-1 rounded-sm transition-shadow"
+            style={{
+              backgroundColor: `hsl(${p.hue} 100% 50%)`,
+              boxShadow: hue === p.hue ? "0 0 0 1.5px hsl(220 10% 80%)" : "none",
+            }}
+          />
+        ))}
+
+        {/* Reset */}
+        <button
+          onClick={onReset}
+          title={`Reset to ${DEFAULT_HUE}°`}
+          className="h-5 w-5 flex-none grid place-items-center rounded-sm transition-opacity"
+          style={{ opacity: isDefault ? 0.25 : 0.7 }}
+        >
+          <svg width="12" height="12" viewBox="0 0 12 12" fill="none" className="text-[hsl(220_10%_60%)]">
+            <path
+              d="M2 2v3h3M10 10V7H7"
+              stroke="currentColor"
+              strokeWidth="1.2"
+              strokeLinecap="round"
+              strokeLinejoin="round"
+            />
+            <path
+              d="M9.5 4.5A4 4 0 0 0 3.17 2.83L2 4M2.5 7.5A4 4 0 0 0 8.83 9.17L10 8"
+              stroke="currentColor"
+              strokeWidth="1.2"
+              strokeLinecap="round"
+              strokeLinejoin="round"
+            />
+          </svg>
+        </button>
+      </div>
+
+      {/* Gradient bar */}
+      <div
+        ref={barRef}
+        className="h-6 rounded-sm cursor-crosshair relative"
+        style={{
+          background: "linear-gradient(to right, hsl(0 100% 50%), hsl(60 100% 50%), hsl(120 100% 50%), hsl(180 100% 50%), hsl(240 100% 50%), hsl(300 100% 50%), hsl(360 100% 50%))",
+        }}
+        onPointerDown={onPointerDown}
+        onPointerMove={onPointerMove}
+      >
+        <div
+          className="absolute top-0 h-full w-1 -translate-x-1/2 border border-white rounded-sm pointer-events-none"
+          style={{
+            left: `${(hue / 360) * 100}%`,
+            backgroundColor: `hsl(${hue} 100% 50%)`,
+          }}
+        />
+      </div>
+    </div>
+  );
+}
+

 export function AboutButton() {
   const [open, setOpen] = useState(false);

     <Dialog.Root open={open} onOpenChange={setOpen}>
